title: How to Use Chopsticks in Japan — and What Never to Do
excerpt: Using chopsticks correctly in Japan is less about technique and more about knowing the four things that are genuinely offensive. The technique is optional. The four things are not.
heroImageAlt: A pair of wooden chopsticks resting on a ceramic chopstick rest beside a bowl of rice
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The four things you must not do
paragraphs: Tatehashi: standing chopsticks upright in a bowl of rice. This is how rice is offered to the dead at funerals. It is considered deeply inauspicious at a dining table. If you need to put your chopsticks down, use the rest (hashioki), or rest them across the bowl edge. Watashi-hashi: resting chopsticks across the top of a bowl as a signal you are done. This is acceptable in practice, but the correct signal is to set them on the hashioki. Sashi-hashi: spearing food with a chopstick as if it were a fork. For things that are difficult to pick up, ask for a fork — it's available at most restaurants. Mayoi-hashi: hovering chopsticks over dishes while deciding what to take. Pick up from the dish you are pointing at.

sections.heading: Passing food chopstick to chopstick
paragraphs: Passing food directly from one person's chopsticks to another's is associated with the funeral ritual of passing cremated bones. If you want to share food, place it on the other person's plate. This rule is known to most Japanese people and explained to children; seeing foreigners do it is understood as unfamiliarity rather than disrespect, but it is worth knowing.

sections.heading: Technique is not the point
paragraphs: Many restaurants that serve foreign visitors have forks available on request. If you cannot use chopsticks comfortably, ask for a fork. Nobody will be offended. Struggling badly with chopsticks for an entire meal while declining a fork is not a gesture of cultural respect — it is inconvenient for you and creates mild concern for the people eating with you. Use the fork. Practice the chopsticks when you have time and aren't hungry.

sections.heading: Picking up communal dishes
paragraphs: At a shared meal — izakaya, shabu-shabu, hot pot — there may be serving chopsticks (toribashi) for transferring food from the central dish to your plate. If there aren't, the convention is to use the reverse end of your own chopsticks (the end you haven't had in your mouth) for serving. This is understood and widely practiced in informal settings.

sections.heading: Disposable chopsticks
paragraphs: Waribashi are the disposable wooden chopsticks served at most casual restaurants and convenience stores. You split them apart from their joined end. Rubbing them together to remove splinters is considered poor form at a proper restaurant — it implies the chopsticks are of low quality, which implies the restaurant is of low quality. At a convenience store eating lunch on a bench, this rule is not in operation. Context applies.

title: The Rules of Gift Giving in Japan: What to Give, How to Give It, When Not To
excerpt: Gift giving in Japan is governed by a set of conventions that are invisible until you get them wrong. Here is what they are.
heroImageAlt: An elegantly wrapped Japanese gift box with a noshi ribbon on a wooden surface
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The presentation matters as much as the gift
paragraphs: In Japan, the wrapping of a gift is not separate from the gift — it is part of it. A well-chosen item presented unwrapped or in a plastic bag conveys carelessness. A modest item in beautiful wrapping conveys consideration. Department stores in Japan wrap purchases without being asked, and the wrapping is typically done with a precision that takes skill. If you are bringing a gift from abroad, find a way to present it wrapped. The intent is not to deceive about value; it is to communicate that you thought about the presentation.

sections.heading: Numbers and colors to avoid
paragraphs: Avoid gifts in sets of four (shi sounds like the word for death) or nine (ku sounds like the word for suffering). Sets of three, five, or seven are considered auspicious. White wrapping paper, used in funeral contexts, and black and white ribbon combinations are better avoided for celebratory gifts. Red is generally acceptable. Gold is particularly positive.

sections.heading: Gifts are not opened in front of the giver
paragraphs: The Japanese convention is to receive a gift graciously, set it aside, and open it privately later. Opening a gift immediately in front of the person who gave it — examining it, exclaiming over it, asking about the price — is not standard. If a Japanese host opens your gift immediately, it is a gesture of warmth toward you specifically. If they set it aside, do not interpret this as indifference. The gift has been received and will be appreciated; the appreciation is simply expressed differently.

sections.heading: Giving with two hands
paragraphs: Gifts, business cards, money envelopes, and any item given as a gesture of significance should be presented with both hands, accompanied by a slight bow. Receiving should be the same. This is not always possible with large items, but the gesture of offering both hands signals respect and intention. Handing something with one hand while doing something else with the other is fine in a casual context and reads as slightly inattentive in a formal one.

sections.heading: What makes a good gift
paragraphs: The best gifts from abroad: regional foods that can't be bought in Japan, quality items associated with your home country, or something that reflects genuine thought about the recipient. Luxury goods as gifts place the recipient in an uncomfortable position (they feel obligated to reciprocate at an equivalent level). Practical consumables — food, drink, quality cosmetics — avoid this dynamic. The rule of thumb used by Japanese people for omiyage applies equally here: regional, edible, individually portioned, appropriately priced.

sections.heading: When not to give a gift
paragraphs: Do not tip in lieu of a gift. Do not give a gift to someone in a customer service role unless you have an established personal relationship with them. Do not give a gift that clearly exceeds the social context — a very expensive present to a casual acquaintance places an obligation on them that is uncomfortable. The gift economy in Japan operates on calibration: the value of the gift reflects the relationship, and mismatching either direction creates awkwardness.

title: Tattoos and Onsen in Japan: What the Policy Actually Is
excerpt: Most onsen ban tattoos. Fewer than you'd think actually enforce it. Here is how to navigate it honestly.
heroImageAlt: Steam rising from an outdoor onsen rotenburo surrounded by autumn trees
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why the ban exists
paragraphs: The tattoo ban at Japanese onsen traces to the historical association between full-body tattoos (irezumi) and organized crime. The ban was introduced not as a cultural statement about tattoos generally but as a practical measure to keep criminal organizations out of communal bathing facilities. The policy has remained for decades largely through institutional inertia, though attitudes have shifted significantly since the Tokyo Olympics announcement in 2013 brought the issue into public debate.

sections.heading: The official policy vs practice
paragraphs: Official policy at most large resort onsen and hotel spa facilities: no tattoos, regardless of size. Enforcement varies significantly. At a small neighborhood sento (public bath) run by a family for 40 years, nobody is checking. At a large facility near a major tourist area, staff may ask at the entrance. The primary concern has always been the communal nature of the bathing — a heavily tattooed person in a visible position in a shared bath creates discomfort for other guests who have the historical association in mind.

sections.heading: Options if you have tattoos
paragraphs: Private baths (kashikiri buro or kashi-kiriburo): many ryokan and onsen hotels offer private family baths bookable by the hour — a full bath for your group only, with no other guests and therefore no policy. This is often the most relaxing option regardless. Tattoo-friendly facilities: they exist and are increasing in number. Websites like Tattoo-Friendly Japan (search for this specifically) maintain updated lists of facilities with explicit tattoo-welcome policies. Covering: some facilities allow tattoos covered by a waterproof bandage or tattoo cover sheet (sold at some facilities), but this is only workable for small tattoos and not universal.

sections.heading: The honest approach
paragraphs: The most straightforward path: contact any onsen facility you intend to visit in advance and ask. Most facilities have dealt with this question before and can give you a direct answer. Arriving at a facility and discovering the policy on entry, then having a confrontation at the desk, is unpleasant for everyone. The honest approach — ask first, respect the answer — is the correct one regardless of your personal opinion about the policy.

sections.heading: Changing attitudes
paragraphs: Japan's tattoo culture is genuinely changing. Younger Japanese people increasingly have tattoos. The Japan Tourism Agency has encouraged onsen facilities to reconsider blanket bans in favor of more nuanced approaches, particularly given international visitor numbers. Some facilities now operate on a no-visible-tattoo basis rather than a full ban. The situation in 2026 is meaningfully different from 2015, and will continue to evolve. The number of tattoo-friendly facilities available is increasing every year.

title: Meishi: The Japanese Business Card Ritual and Why It Matters
excerpt: In Japan, the business card is not a piece of paper. It is a physical representation of the person. Treating it as stationary is the fastest way to start a professional relationship poorly.
heroImageAlt: A Japanese business card (meishi) held with two hands against a wooden meeting table
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What a business card represents
paragraphs: In Japan, the meishi (business card) exchange is a ritual that establishes who you are, your position, and your company — in one gesture, at the start of every professional interaction. Cards are not handed out casually. They are exchanged at the beginning of a meeting with a formality that can feel significant to those not used to it. Understanding why makes the ritual easier: the card is standing in for a formal introduction that would otherwise take several minutes of verbal exchange.

sections.heading: How to present your card
paragraphs: Hold the card with both hands, text facing the recipient so they can read it immediately, and offer a slight bow as you present it. Say your name and company as you do. If you are handing out cards in a group, the most senior person goes first. Do not hand out cards across a table — walk around or hand directly.

sections.heading: How to receive a card
paragraphs: Receive the card with both hands. Take a moment to read it — not a cursory glance, but actually look at it. Acknowledge the person's name and company. If you are sitting down at a meeting, place the card on the table in front of you in order of seniority (most senior person's card nearest you, others in the order they were received). Do not put the card in your pocket or wallet until the meeting is over. The card on the table means the person is still present in the meeting, literally and symbolically.

sections.heading: What not to do
paragraphs: Do not write on a business card in front of the person who gave it to you. Do not fold it. Do not put it in your back pocket and sit on it. Do not place anything on top of it (not a cup, not another card). These actions read as treating the card — and by extension the person — carelessly. After the meeting, file cards appropriately. Japanese professionals use meishi holders specifically for this purpose.

sections.heading: If you don't have cards
paragraphs: Arriving at a Japanese business meeting without cards is not a catastrophe, but it is noticed. A brief explanation — 'I apologize, I have run out of cards' — is appropriate. Most Japanese businesspeople will accept this gracefully. If you are planning any professional interactions in Japan, have cards printed. The Japanese side will often have English on the reverse, and printing the same for your own card is appreciated.

title: How to Order and Drink Sake in Japan Without Getting It Wrong
excerpt: Sake is Japan's most misunderstood drink. Most people drink it too warm, in the wrong glass, with the wrong food. This is how to do it correctly.
heroImageAlt: A small ceramic tokkuri flask and ochoko cups of sake on a wooden table at an izakaya
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Sake is not always drunk hot
paragraphs: The most persistent misunderstanding about sake outside Japan is that it is drunk hot. Sake is drunk at a range of temperatures — cold (reishu), room temperature (jouon), slightly warm (nuru-kan), hot (atsu-kan) — and the temperature used depends on the type of sake and personal preference. Premium ginjo and daiginjo sake are almost always drunk cold or at room temperature; heating them diminishes the delicate aromatics that justify their price. Warmer temperatures suit rougher, more robust honjozo or futsushu. At a good sake restaurant, asking which temperature is recommended for a specific bottle is a reasonable question and will get a thoughtful answer.

sections.heading: You pour for others, not yourself
paragraphs: The social convention around sake at a table is that you pour for others and they pour for you. You do not fill your own cup. If someone's cup is empty and you have the carafe, pour for them. If your cup is empty, hold it up slightly toward whoever has the carafe — this is the signal. At a very informal dinner with close friends this rule is relaxed; at any formal or semi-formal context it applies. Pouring for yourself first is read as either self-centered or unaware of the convention.

sections.heading: The receiving gesture
paragraphs: When someone pours sake for you, you do not just hold the cup. You hold it with both hands, or support it from below with one hand while holding it with the other. This is the equivalent of receiving a gift with both hands — a gesture that says you are receiving attentively. When the pour is done, you drink (or set the cup down), and then you pour in return.

sections.heading: What to order
paragraphs: At an izakaya, the standard entry point is a carafe (tokkuri) rather than a bottle. Carafes come in small (1 go, about 180ml) and large (2 go, 360ml) sizes. The small carafe is the right starting order while you are deciding. For a meal at a sake specialist or a good Japanese restaurant, the menu will have sake listed with classifications (junmai, ginjo, daiginjo). Ask for osusume (what do you recommend) and specify whether you prefer lighter or richer, fruity or dry. The staff will know.

sections.heading: Kanpai
paragraphs: The toast is kanpai. Eyes contact, glasses meet lightly (for sake cups, tap gently — they are fragile), drink at the same time. It is customary to wait for kanpai before drinking, even if the sake arrives before everyone is served. Making eye contact during kanpai is not optional — it is the point of the gesture. Looking away while toasting is the universal bad-omen equivalent and works the same in Japan as anywhere else.

title: The Japanese Breakfast: What It Is and Why It Will Change Your Morning
excerpt: A traditional Japanese breakfast is not a quick meal. It is a composed sequence of small things done well, and eating it once will make every hotel breakfast you eat for the rest of your life feel like a concession.
heroImageAlt: A traditional Japanese breakfast set with rice, miso soup, grilled fish, pickles, and tofu
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What a Japanese breakfast is
paragraphs: A traditional Japanese breakfast (washoku asa-gohan) consists of: steamed white rice, miso soup (with tofu, wakame, and green onion), a piece of grilled fish (typically salmon, mackerel, or sanma), a small selection of pickled vegetables (tsukemono), a square of tofu, a small egg dish (tamagoyaki or onsen tamago), and nori (dried seaweed). Everything arrives simultaneously. Nothing is sweet. Coffee is available separately, usually afterward. This is the standard morning meal across ryokan, traditional hotels, and the home kitchens of people who cook breakfast.

sections.heading: The grilled fish
paragraphs: The grilled fish is the anchor. A properly grilled piece of sake (salmon) or saba (mackerel) — salted and grilled slowly over charcoal or in a specialized fish broiler — has a crisp skin, slightly smoky interior, and a flavor that is to a hotel breakfast buffet as a proper espresso is to an office coffee machine. At a ryokan, this fish was probably grilled minutes before it arrived. Eat it immediately.

sections.heading: Miso soup
paragraphs: Miso soup at a proper breakfast is made from dashi (usually kombu and katsuobushi stock) and miso paste, not from a powder. The difference is apparent. The soup arrives hot in a lacquerware bowl with a lid. Lift the lid, place it on the side inverted, drink directly from the bowl, or use the small spoon provided. Pouring the miso soup over your rice is acceptable in informal settings. Mixing the rice with everything into a kind of porridge — ochazuke-style, though that's technically green tea poured over rice — is a personal approach that some people find comforting.

sections.heading: Where to eat one
paragraphs: The best Japanese breakfast is the one at a ryokan, where it arrives in your room or a dedicated dining room and takes 45 minutes to eat properly. The second-best is at a traditional Japanese hotel (a business hotel that has opted to serve Japanese breakfast rather than a buffet). The kissaten morning set — coffee, toast, sometimes a small egg — is a different, simpler thing entirely. The convenience store breakfast is useful and fast and not the same experience.

sections.heading: What it teaches you
paragraphs: Eating a Japanese breakfast once — properly, without hurrying — resets certain assumptions about what mornings can be. The meal is light enough that you feel alert afterward rather than heavy. The flavors are savory and complex at 7am in a way that feels unusual until it feels obvious. The effort that went into it is visible in every dish. For many visitors, the ryokan breakfast is the meal they remember longest from the trip. Arrive at the dining room at the scheduled time, sit down properly, and give it the 45 minutes it is asking for.

title: The Best Time to Visit Japan: An Honest Month-by-Month Guide
excerpt: Spring cherry blossoms and autumn foliage are spectacular. They are also the two most crowded periods of the year. Here is what every month actually offers.
heroImageAlt: Mount Fuji reflected in a lake surrounded by summer wildflowers
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: January–February: winter, crowds at minimum
paragraphs: January is Japan's quietest month for tourism. After New Year's (January 1–3, busy with domestic travel), the country empties. Hotels are cheaper. Shinkansen seats are available. Famous sites are accessible at human scale. The weather in Tokyo and Kyoto is cold but manageable — 4–10°C, mostly clear. Onsen towns are at their peak: Hakone, Kinosaki, Beppu. February adds Sapporo's Snow Festival (first week), one of Japan's most-attended winter events. Good months for people who want the experience without the crowd premium.

sections.heading: March–April: cherry blossoms
paragraphs: Sakura season typically runs late March to mid-April depending on location and year. Tokyo usually peaks in late March; Kyoto about a week later; northern Japan and higher elevations into late April. The flowers last around one to two weeks at any given location. This is the most visited season, the most expensive, and the most beautiful. Book accommodation four to six months in advance. The parks and famous viewpoints are genuinely crowded. The less-visited spots — riverside embankments, school paths, neighborhood parks — offer the same flowers with a fraction of the people.

sections.heading: May: the sweet spot
paragraphs: Late May (after Golden Week, which runs April 29–May 5) is one of the best times to visit Japan. Spring is still present. Temperatures are comfortable — 15–22°C in most of central Japan. The golden week crowds have gone. Hotels return to normal pricing. The greenery is at its freshest. May 10–30 is consistently underrated.

sections.heading: June–August: summer and humidity
paragraphs: June brings the rainy season (tsuyu) to most of Honshu — two to four weeks of grey skies and intermittent rain that is frustrating for outdoor sightseeing. July and August are hot and humid: 30–36°C in Tokyo and Osaka with 80%+ humidity. Summer festivals (matsuri) are spectacular but the weather is punishing. Hokkaido is the exception — cool, green, and genuinely pleasant in July and August. If you must visit in summer, either go to Hokkaido or plan your days around early mornings and air-conditioned afternoons.

sections.heading: September–November: autumn foliage
paragraphs: September brings typhoon season risk (primarily September–October) and the beginning of more comfortable temperatures. By October the weather is excellent: clear days, 15–25°C, minimal rain. Autumn foliage (koyo) peaks in November — Hokkaido in early October, central Honshu through November. The period from late October through mid-November is the second most visited after cherry blossom season, and prices reflect this. Kyoto in November is busy to the point of requiring strategic planning around early mornings and weekdays.

sections.heading: The recommendation
paragraphs: For a first visit with no fixed seasonal preference: late May or mid-October. For a visit specifically structured around a natural event: cherry blossoms in late March–early April (plan far ahead), autumn leaves in early-mid November. For minimum crowds and maximum value: January or June (accepting the weather trade-offs of each). The worst time to book without planning is Golden Week (late April–early May) or the first week of August. Not because they're bad — but because accommodation disappears a year in advance and prices reflect demand.

title: Taking a Taxi in Japan: What to Know Before You Get In
excerpt: Japanese taxis are expensive, exceptionally clean, and almost entirely driven by people who know exactly where they are going. The main things to know are not about navigation.
heroImageAlt: A black Toyota Crown Japanese taxi cab waiting at a Tokyo intersection at night
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The door opens automatically
paragraphs: Japanese taxi doors open and close automatically, controlled by the driver. Do not touch the door. Attempting to open or close it manually will work but is unnecessary and slightly startles the driver. This catches almost every first-time visitor. Stand near the door, wait for it to open, get in. When you arrive, the driver will open it for you. Get out, step away, the door closes.

sections.heading: Show the destination in writing
paragraphs: Most taxi drivers in Japan speak limited English. The reliable approach: have your destination written in Japanese characters, either on your phone (from Google Maps, copy the Japanese name) or on a piece of paper from your hotel. Pronunciation of Western place names in Japanese is often different enough from English pronunciation to cause confusion. A screen showing the map with the destination pinned is universally understood. Your hotel's front desk should always be able to provide you with a card showing the hotel address in Japanese — ask for one at check-in.

sections.heading: Fares and metering
paragraphs: Fares are metered. In Tokyo, the base fare is around ¥500–550 for the first 1.2 kilometers, then ¥100 per approximately 300–400 meters. A 5-kilometer city ride typically runs ¥1,500–2,500. Late-night surcharges (after 10pm or 11pm depending on company) add 20–30%. There is no tip. Pay exactly what the meter says. Credit cards are accepted in most Tokyo taxis now; IC cards (Suica) are accepted in many. Check the payment stickers on the rear side window before you get in if you're unsure.

sections.heading: When to use a taxi
paragraphs: Taxis are worth the cost in specific situations: after the last train, with heavy luggage between hotel and station, when traveling with elderly or young children, in cities with infrequent bus service, or when the destination is not near a train line. In Tokyo during normal hours, taxis are significantly more expensive than the train and not faster — traffic density means trains win on time and price for most inner-city routes. Outside Tokyo, in smaller cities, taxis become more valuable as transit alternatives are thinner.

sections.heading: Hailing and apps
paragraphs: Taxis in Japan are hailed from the street — stand at the curb, extend your arm, and a cab with a lit red indicator (available for hire) will stop. Do not try to hail a cab at a zebra crossing or intersection. Taxi apps: GO (Japan-specific, major cities, English available), Uber (operates in some Japanese cities but uses licensed taxis rather than private drivers), and DiDi. GO is the most widely used and works in Tokyo, Osaka, Kyoto, and most other large cities. Having it installed before you need it at midnight is a better plan than finding it at midnight.

title: Matsumoto: The Castle Town That Gets Everything Right
excerpt: Two and a half hours from Tokyo, a city with one of Japan's three great original castles, a mountain backdrop, an excellent food scene, and a fraction of Kyoto's visitor numbers. There is no explanation for why more people don't come here.
heroImageAlt: Matsumoto Castle reflected in the surrounding moat on a clear autumn morning with the Japanese Alps behind
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The castle
paragraphs: Matsumoto Castle is one of Japan's twelve surviving original castles — meaning it was not destroyed, bombed, or rebuilt in concrete in the postwar era. The main tower (tenshu) dates to 1593. It is black, which is unusual (most Japanese castles are white), and it sits in a moat surrounded by mountains that the castle architects clearly intended as a backdrop. In spring there are cherry trees around the moat. In autumn there are maple trees. In winter there is snow on the Alps. In summer the castle is reflected in the moat in the early morning before the crowds arrive. Every season offers something specific. Entrance is ¥700.

sections.heading: Inside the castle
paragraphs: The interior is original — steep wooden staircases (more ladder than stair in the upper floors), low beams, the smell of old timber, defensive features like gun ports and stone-drop windows. It is small enough to feel like a real building rather than a museum installation, and large enough to spend an hour in. The views from the top floor extend across the city to the Alps. The interior can be crowded at peak times; arrive at 8:30am when it opens and you will often have the upper floors largely to yourself.

sections.heading: The old town
paragraphs: The Nakamachi and Kaichi districts preserve Edo-period kura (storehouses) that have been converted into craft shops, sake breweries, coffee shops, and restaurants. The streets are genuinely old and genuinely in use — not a preserved historical theme zone but a working neighborhood that has been lived in continuously. The Kaichi School building (1876), built in a hybrid Japanese-Western style unique to Meiji-era architecture, is worth the five-minute detour.

sections.heading: Food and sake
paragraphs: Matsumoto is in Nagano Prefecture, which produces excellent sake, buckwheat, and mountain vegetables. The soba in Matsumoto is made from local buckwheat and is a different thing from the Tokyo variety — coarser, darker, more robust. Mameichi, a small soba restaurant near the castle, is consistently recommended. The local sake breweries in Nakamachi offer tastings. Basho, a specialist sake bar near the station, has one of the better regional sake selections in the area.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: From Tokyo (Shinjuku): the Azusa Limited Express to Matsumoto takes 2h30m, costs around ¥6,500 one-way, and is covered by JR Pass. From Nagoya: 2h by Shinano Limited Express. The city is small enough to walk everywhere once you arrive — the castle is 15 minutes from the station on foot. A single full day is enough for the castle, old town, and a proper meal. Two days allows excursions to Kamikochi (30 minutes by bus, one of Japan's most beautiful alpine valleys) or Hotaka (20 minutes by train, a shrine with an unusual collection of religious art).

sections.heading: The Alps in winter
paragraphs: Matsumoto in winter — December through February — is exceptional if you are prepared for cold. The Alps are fully snowcapped. The castle moat reflects a stark black tower against white mountains on clear mornings. Visitor numbers are low. The onsen around the city (Asama, Utsukushigahara, Shirahone) are at their best in cold weather. Shirahone Onsen specifically, 90 minutes by bus from Matsumoto, is a small valley onsen village that has barely changed since the Edo period and is close to empty outside of August.

title: Kyoto in Three Days: What to Actually See and What to Skip
excerpt: Kyoto has over 1,600 temples and shrines. You will not see them all. Here is how to choose the ones that matter — and what makes them worth choosing over the others.
heroImageAlt: The vermilion torii gates of Fushimi Inari at dawn with no crowds visible
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Day one: the east side
paragraphs: Start at Fushimi Inari. The torii gate path that runs up Mt. Inari is the most photographed image in Japan, and the photographs don't exaggerate it. The path continues for four kilometers past the main tourist zone, thinning to almost nothing by the second kilometer. Arrive at 6:30am or earlier and walk past the first major platform — the mountain path above it is genuinely quiet until 9am. In the afternoon: Kinkaku-ji (the Golden Pavilion). Arrive between 2pm and closing. It is always crowded; go anyway, because the building and its reflection in the pond are one of those things that maintains its impact despite the crowds. This is rarer than it sounds.

sections.heading: Day one: evening in Gion
paragraphs: After 5pm, walk through Gion. The most famous streets (Hanamikoji, Shimbashi) are genuinely beautiful in the evening light. In summer (July), the Gion Matsuri preparations fill the streets with yamahoko floats. In autumn, the maple trees along Shimbashi turn. Come to look, not to photograph — or at minimum, look before you photograph. The neighborhood has been fighting the worst effects of mass tourism for several years, and the experience of being there is different when you are present rather than performing.

sections.heading: Day two: the northwest
paragraphs: Arashiyama is the west side's major district, accessible by the Sagano Scenic Railway or the Randen tram. The bamboo grove is a ten-minute walk from the Arashiyama station and genuinely spectacular — and very crowded by 10am, nearly empty at 7am. After the grove: Tenryu-ji, one of Kyoto's great Zen gardens, with a pond framed by borrowed scenery (shakkei) of the mountains behind it. In autumn the maple trees around the pond are what the photographs in every Japan feature show. After Tenryu-ji, walk the Sagano path north to Jojakko-ji — a moss-covered temple on a hillside that appears in almost no itineraries and is about as far from the crowd as you can get while staying in Arashiyama.

sections.heading: Day two: Nishiki Market
paragraphs: Return to central Kyoto in the afternoon for Nishiki Market — a covered arcade called Kyoto's Kitchen, narrow, parallel to Shijo Street, with stalls selling pickled vegetables, grilled mochi, tofu, Kyoto-style dashi components, fresh yuba, and specialty food items. Eat as you walk (this is one of the exceptions to the no-eating-while-walking rule, since the market format makes stationary eating difficult). The market closes early — most stalls done by 6pm.

sections.heading: Day three: what most itineraries miss
paragraphs: Philosopher's Path (Tetsugaku no Michi): a two-kilometer canal-side walkway between Ginkaku-ji and Nanzen-ji in the northeastern hills. In cherry blossom season the canal is lined with sakura. At any time of year it is quieter than the central tourist circuit and lined with small cafés, tofu restaurants, and craft shops. Walk it slowly, south to north. At the northern end: Nanzen-ji, a Zen temple complex with an aqueduct running through the grounds — one of the odder visual combinations in Kyoto and one of the most memorable.

sections.heading: What to skip
paragraphs: Nijo Castle: historically interesting, architecturally significant (the nightingale floor squeaks deliberately to detect intruders), and fairly dry as a visitor experience compared to everything else Kyoto offers. Come if you have four or more days. Toei Kyoto Studio Park (a movie set theme park): enthusiastically recommended by some and entirely optional for everyone who isn't specifically interested in period samurai dramas. Philosopher's Path during peak cherry blossom season: beautiful, but the crowd density removes the contemplative quality that is the entire point.
